title: QUIC CRT server client-auth transcript/runtime prerequisite architecture
purpose: Describe the smallest internal managed server seam needed to emit TLS 1.3 CertificateRequest and accept client Certificate / CertificateVerify / Finished through the existing transcript/runtime bridge without widening the public listener boundary yet.
risks: If the server-auth flag is not threaded into the transcript owner before handshake processing starts, the managed branch will still look client-only even though the code compiles.
If the client-certificate callback is modeled as a trust-store replacement, the slice will overclaim PKI behavior that was explicitly deferred.
If the public validator is relaxed before the internal runtime branch is proven, the supported boundary will widen without an honest proof plan.
design_summary: The narrowest honest step is an internal server-auth prerequisite inside the existing TLS transcript and bridge machinery. The transcript owner needs a role-aware branch for an inbound client Certificate / CertificateVerify / Finished sequence after ClientHello when server client-auth is enabled, the key schedule needs to emit one local CertificateRequest and verify client CertificateVerify with the client-side TLS 1.3 context string, and the transport bridge needs a server-side validation callback seam that can accept or reject the presented client certificate without introducing a broader PKI policy surface. The public server carrier remains unchanged in this slice, so the current validator rejection can stay in place while the internal prerequisite is proven.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This architecture slice is intentionally narrower than the eventual public server-auth floor. It covers the internal transcript/runtime prerequisite only: CertificateRequest emission, client Certificate / CertificateVerify / Finished acceptance, and callback-driven acceptance on the existing bridge seam.
